# SCHEDULE 5

## PROJECT DESCRIPTION, MILESTONES AND BUDGET INFORMATION

### Project Title: Pacific Salmon Explorer

#### 1. Overview and Project Description

##### 1.1. Project overview

Since 2008, the PSF has collaborated with First Nations, the BC provincial government, DFO, and other experts to develop a standardized approach for compiling the information needed to assess the status of salmon populations and evaluate pressures on their spawning, rearing, and migratory habitats. Our approach involves working directly with local communities and regional experts through the establishment of regional Technical Advisory Committees (TACs). These committees help to build broad support for our work as well as provide a mechanism for integrating expert knowledge into our assessment work. This collaborative and inclusive approach has allowed us to secure the support and participation from more than a dozen First Nations throughout North and Central BC in our assessments for the Nass and Skeena River watersheds. Most recently, we have partnered with the Gitxaala, Haisla, Kitasoo/Xais’Xai, Huitusk, Nuxalk, and Wuikinuxv First Nations to expand our assessments to watersheds of the Central Coast and communicate the results via the Pacific Salmon Explorer.

The Pacific Salmon Explorer (PSE) (www.salmonexplorer.ca) is an online data visualization tool that was launched in 2016. It allows people interested in salmon conservation and management to gain better access to baseline data relevant to Pacific salmon. Using this innovative platform, the public can explore salmon data through interactive maps and figures as well as print status reports for all geographically and genetically distinct Pacific salmon populations (known as Conservation Units (CUs) under Canada’s Wild Salmon Policy). Currently, content is limited to Skeena River salmon populations, with Nass River information under development.

This project proposes to scale out the Pacific Salmon Explorer to two other important salmon-bearing watersheds in BC: (1) the Fraser River and its key tributaries, and (2) other major rivers draining into the Strait of Georgia and the West Coast of Vancouver Island. By compiling biological data for salmon populations in the coastal watersheds of southern BC, and quantifying the spatial extent and intensity of pressures on their habitats, this project will help to identify priority areas for coastal restoration and support the development of strategies for mitigating key threats and pressures that may be impeding the recovery of important salmon populations such as Southern BC Chinook. By facilitating the conservation and recovery of salmon CUs in BC’s coastal watersheds, this project will also support recovery efforts for many other marine species, including threatened and endangered Northern and Southern Resident Killer Whales, who rely on abundant salmon populations (in particular, Chinook and chum salmon from southern BC) for their survival and recovery.

##### 1.2. Project Description

In order to achieve our project objectives and expand our work to southern BC watersheds, the PSF will apply the same methodologies used to assess the status of salmon CUs and habitat pressures in the Skeena River, Nass River, and Central Coast watersheds to the Fraser-Thompson and South Coast – Vancouver Island regions. A critical first step for this project will involve identifying and subsequently compiling the best available spatial data sets on land-use pressures and relevant salmon datasets (e.g. catch, spawner abundance) from federal, provincial and First Nations governments, industry, non-governmental organizations, researchers, and other groups. The compiled datasets will be housed within the PSF’s Salmon Database and metadata will be generated for all compiled information. Using the assembled datasets, we will assess the biological status of each CU within the identified regions using two commonly applied metrics (based on historic spawner abundance and based on the shape of the stock recruitment relationship). This exercise will provide a first-cut assessment of the biological status of salmon CUs in southern BC watersheds as well as a coarse-scale overview of major human and environmental pressures on their freshwater habitats.

prioritize locations for restoration projects. In addition, over time, the Pacific Salmon Explorer will evolve into a platform for displaying the results of restoration investments.

Furthermore, the Pacific Salmon Explorer will support efforts to recover threatened and endangered Northern and Southern Resident Killer Whales, which rely on Chinook and chum salmon as a key food source. The information displayed in the Pacific Salmon Explorer can be used to track changes in abundance of Chinook and chum CUs that are important prey and also identify, and ultimately mitigate, cumulative pressures on the spawning and rearing habitats for these CUs. **Milestone 3: Central Coast – completion of analytical work**

**Estimated Cost CRF $125,000**

**Estimated Cost Other Funding Sources $141,155**

**Start and End dates: 06-Apr-2017 to 31-Dec-2017**

**General Description of the Milestone:**

This milestone encompasses all activities related to the analysis and assessment of 116 Central Coast Conservation Units and pressures on their freshwater habitats. This includes working with Technical Advisory Committees and project partners who guide the analytical work.

The following will be produced as part of this milestone:

1. A repository of vetted datasets related to pressures on freshwater salmon habitats in the Central Coast, including improved datasets for spawning locations.
2. A core set of habitat pressure indicators and defined benchmark values to assess the risks posted to salmon habitats from human and environmental pressures.
3. A suite of datasets showing low, moderate, or high risk of habitat degradation form individual habitat pressures, as well as cumulative risk from all pressures combined, for 116 Conservation Units in the Central Coast.
4. A suite of datasets related to the status of 116 Conservation Units in the Central Coast, including spawner abundance, catch and run size, trends in spawner abundance, spawner surveys by stream, run-timing, recruits-per-spawner, and biological status.
5. A technical report that describes the methodologies used to assess salmon Conservation Units and their freshwater habitats in the Central Coast.

**Tasks:**

1. Develop a schedule of activities and define expected partner contributions specific to this milestone.
2. Engagement with two Technical Advisory Committees including technical workshops, one-on-one engagement, and community visits. The Technical Advisory Committees include technical representatives from the Gitxaala, Haisla, Kitasoo/’Xai’xais, Heilsuk, Nuxalk, and Wuikinuxv First Nations as well as regional DFO staff. The northern Technical Advisory Committee will meet in Prince Rupert and/or Terrace and the southern Technical Advisory Committee will meet in Vancouver. Both Technical Advisory Committees will meet three times through the duration of the Central Coast project.
3. Data compilation for population and habitat assessments, including documenting local knowledge of spawning locations.
4. Development of habitat indicators and benchmarks.
5. Completion of draft habitat risk assessments for spawning and migratory habitats for all 116 salmon Conservation Units.
6. Development of abundance-based benchmarks for all 116 salmon Conservation Units.
7. Quantify the biological status of all 116 salmon Conservation Units.
8. Create documentation of assessment methodologies.

**Expected Results:**

1. A technical report that describes the methodologies used to assess salmon Conservation Units and their freshwater habitats in the Central Coast, which includes a list of the datasets, indicators, and benchmark values included in the analysis

## Annual Work Plan for 2018-19

The following will be produced as part of this milestone:

- A repository of vetted datasets related to pressures on freshwater salmon habitats in the Fraser-Thompson region, including improved datasets for spawning locations
- A core set of habitat pressure indicators and defined benchmark values to assess the risks posted to salmon habitats from human and environmental pressures.
- A suite of datasets showing low, moderate, or high risk of habitat degradation form individual habitat pressures, as well as cumulative risk from all pressures combined, for over 50 Conservation Units in the Fraser-Thompson region.
- A suite of datasets related to the status of over 50 Conservation Units in the Fraser-Thompson region, including spawner abundance, catch and run size, trends in spawner abundance, spawner surveys by stream, run-timing, recruits-per-spawner, and biological status.
- A technical report that describes the methodologies used to assess salmon Conservation Units and their freshwater habitats in the Fraser-Thompson region.

1. Develop a schedule of activities and define expected partner contributions specific to this milestone.
2. Engagement with two or three Technical Advisory Committee, including technical workshops, one-on-one engagement, and community visits. Participation in the Technical Advisory Committees will be based on geography, and the workshops will tentatively take place in Prince George, Kamloops, and Vancouver (locations TBC). Each Technical Advisory Committee will have approximately five to ten members and meet two to three times through the duration of the Fraser-Thompson project.
3. Data compilation for population and habitat assessments, including documenting local knowledge of spawning locations.

# SCHEDULE 5

## PROJECT DESCRIPTION, MILESTONES AND BUDGET INFORMATION

### Project Title: Pacific Salmon Explorer

#### 1. Overview and Project Description

##### 1.1. Project overview

Since 2008, the PSF has collaborated with First Nations, the BC provincial government, DFO, and other experts to develop a standardized approach for compiling the information needed to assess the status of salmon populations and evaluate pressures on their spawning, rearing, and migratory habitats. Our approach involves working directly with local communities and regional experts through the establishment of regional Technical Advisory Committees (TACs). These committees help to build broad support for our work as well as provide a mechanism for integrating expert knowledge into our assessment work. This collaborative and inclusive approach has allowed us to secure the support and participation from more than a dozen First Nations throughout North and Central BC in our assessments for the Nass and Skeena River watersheds. Most recently, we have partnered with the Gitxaala, Haisla, Kitasoo/Xais’Xai, Huitusk, Nuxalk, and Wuikinuxv First Nations to expand our assessments to watersheds of the Central Coast and communicate the results via the Pacific Salmon Explorer.

The Pacific Salmon Explorer (PSE) (www.salmonexplorer.ca) is an online data visualization tool that was launched in 2016. It allows people interested in salmon conservation and management to gain better access to baseline data relevant to Pacific salmon. Using this innovative platform, the public can explore salmon data through interactive maps and figures as well as print status reports for all geographically and genetically distinct Pacific salmon populations (known as Conservation Units (CUs) under Canada’s Wild Salmon Policy). Currently, content is limited to Skeena River salmon populations, with Nass River information under development.

This project proposes to scale out the Pacific Salmon Explorer to two other important salmon-bearing watersheds in BC: (1) the Fraser River and its key tributaries, and (2) other major rivers draining into the Strait of Georgia and the West Coast of Vancouver Island. By compiling biological data for salmon populations in the coastal watersheds of southern BC, and quantifying the spatial extent and intensity of pressures on their habitats, this project will help to identify priority areas for coastal restoration and support the development of strategies for mitigating key threats and pressures that may be impeding the recovery of important salmon populations such as Southern BC Chinook. By facilitating the conservation and recovery of salmon CUs in BC’s coastal watersheds, this project will also support recovery efforts for many other marine species, including threatened and endangered Northern and Southern Resident Killer Whales, who rely on abundant salmon populations (in particular, Chinook and chum salmon from southern BC) for their survival and recovery.

##### 1.2. Project Description

In order to achieve our project objectives and expand our work to southern BC watersheds, the PSF will apply the same methodologies used to assess the status of salmon CUs and habitat pressures in the Skeena River, Nass River, and Central Coast watersheds to the Fraser-Thompson and South Coast – Vancouver Island regions. A critical first step for this project will involve identifying and subsequently compiling the best available spatial data sets on land-use pressures and relevant salmon datasets (e.g. catch, spawner abundance) from federal, provincial and First Nations governments, industry, non-governmental organizations, researchers, and other groups. The compiled datasets will be housed within the PSF’s Salmon Database and metadata will be generated for all compiled information. Using the assembled datasets, we will assess the biological status of each CU within the identified regions using two commonly applied metrics (based on historic spawner abundance and based on the shape of the stock recruitment relationship). This exercise will provide a first-cut assessment of the biological status of salmon CUs in southern BC watersheds as well as a coarse-scale overview of major human and environmental pressures on their freshwater habitats.

Page 14 of 48


===== PAGE 2 (f2435abf79_page0002.png) =====
CA No: 17-HPAC-01340
Pacific Salmon Foundation

To undertake the above habitat and population status assessments, we will establish TACs for each of the regions. These TACs will be critical to the successful delivery of this project by helping to (1) ensure that the assessments reflect local, practical and ecological circumstances, (2) identify and provide access to source datasets, (3) ground-truth the assessment results, and (4) provide overall direction on the communication and presentation of the project outputs. The TACs will be comprised of individual First Nations within the Fraser-Thompson and South Coast – Vancouver Island regions, as well as umbrella First Nations organizations such as the First Nations Fisheries Council, the Lower Fraser Fisheries Alliance, and the Upper Fraser Fisheries Alliance, as well as representatives from the DFO, local environmental NGOs, and technical staff from the BC Ministries of Environment and Forests, Lands, and Natural Resource Operations.

The following tasks outline the general approach to completing the technical work required for the various regions comprising the project:

1. Complete population status and habitat pressure assessments for all salmon CUs in the Nass River watershed;
2. Integrate the Nass River assessments into the Pacific Salmon Explorer and release to the public;
3. Complete population status and habitat pressure assessments for salmon CUs that use rivers draining large coastal fjords on the North and Central Coast including the Kitimat River;
4. Integrate the North and Central Coast assessments into the Pacific Salmon Explorer and release to the public;
5. Establish multi-partner TACs for the Fraser/Thompson River and South Coast/Vancouver Island and carry out population status and habitat pressure assessments for salmon CUs by undertaking the following technical work:
   - Identify and map known spawning locations for chum (Onchorynchus keta), Chinook (O. kisutch), coho (O. kisutch), sockeye (O. nerka), and pink (O. gorbuscha) salmon Conservation Units;
   - Synthesize existing datasets on regional pressures known to impact salmon habitats (e.g. road development, mining, forestry);
   - Use a GIS-based approach to quantify the spatial extent and intensity of individual pressures on salmon habitats;
   - Identify which salmon spawning, rearing, and migratory habitats face high, moderate, or low risk of habitat degradation due to cumulative pressures on the landscape;
   - Establish a baseline of scientific data for salmon CUs in the two regions (spawner abundance, catch, run-timing) and apply standardized metrics for assessing the biological status of these CUs.
6. Integrate the Fraser-Thompson and South Coast-Vancouver Island assessments into the Pacific Salmon Explorer and release to the public.

1.3. Outcomes Description

Successful expansion of the Pacific Salmon Explorer beyond the Skeena River, to watersheds of the Nass, Central Coast, Fraser River, and South Coast – Vancouver Island regions will directly support DFO delivery of the Oceans Protection Plan. The expansion of this online data visualization tool will prove to be a valuable resource for integrated fisheries management and restoration of coastal ecosystems by displaying information related to the status of individual salmon CUs and publicly available maps and summary reports that provide a regional-scale overview of pressures on salmon habitats. The Pacific Salmon Explorer provides a much needed synthesis of baseline information that is critical for developing, planning, and monitoring coastal restoration projects. Fisheries and land managers can use the spatially-explicit inventory of aquatic habitats at the highest risk of degradation from cumulative pressures to systematically

Page 15 of 48


===== PAGE 3 (f2435abf79_page0003.png) =====
CA No: 17-HPAC-01340
Pacific Salmon Foundation

prioritize locations for restoration projects. In addition, over time, the Pacific Salmon Explorer will evolve into a platform for displaying the results of restoration investments.

Furthermore, the Pacific Salmon Explorer will support efforts to recover threatened and endangered Northern and Southern Resident Killer Whales, which rely on Chinook and chum salmon as a key food source. The information displayed in the Pacific Salmon Explorer can be used to track changes in abundance of Chinook and chum CUs that are important prey and also identify, and ultimately mitigate, cumulative pressures on the spawning and rearing habitats for these CUs. Both of these actions are considered important strategies for ensuring an adequate and accessible food supply, a key factor in the recovery of Resident Killer Whales.

- results – review analytical outputs and ground-truth results.

The following will be produced as part of this milestone:

- A repository of vetted datasets related to pressures on freshwater salmon habitats in the South Coast-Vancouver Island region, including improved datasets for spawning locations.
- A core set of habitat pressure indicators and defined benchmark values to assess the risks posted to salmon habitats from human and environmental pressures.
- A suite of datasets showing low, moderate, or high risk of habitat degradation from individual habitat pressures, as well as cumulative risk from all pressures combined, for over 90 Conservation Units in the South Coast-Vancouver Island region.
- A suite of datasets related to the status of over 90 Conservation Units in the South Coast-Vancouver Island region, including spawner abundance, catch and run size, trends in spawner abundance, spawner surveys by stream, run-timing, recruits-per-spawner, and biological status.
- A technical report that describes the methodologies used to assess salmon Conservation Units and their freshwater habitats in the South Coast-Vancouver Island region.

**Tasks:**

1. Develop a schedule of activities and define expected partner contributions specific to this milestone
2. Engagement with Technical Advisory Committees, including technical workshops, one-on-one engagement, and community visits. There will likely be two or three Technical Advisory Committees for the South Coast-Vancouver Island; this will be determined in consultation with project partners. Participation in the Technical Advisory Committees will be based on geography, and the workshops will tentatively take place in Victoria and/or Port Alberni, and Vancouver (locations TBC). Each Technical Advisory Committee will have approximately five to ten members and meet two to three times through the duration of the South Coast-Vancouver Island project.
3. Data compilation for population and habitat assessments, including documenting local knowledge of spawning locations.

## Annual Work Plan for 2019-20

4. Development of draft habitat indicators and benchmarks.
5. Completion of habitat risk assessments for spawning and migratory habitats for over 90 salmon Conservation Units.
6. Development of abundance-based benchmarks for over 90 salmon Conservation Units.
7. Quantify the biological status of over 90 salmon Conservation Units.
8. Create documentation of assessment methodologies.

### Expected Results:

1. A technical report that describes the methodologies used to assess salmon Conservation Units and their freshwater habitats in the South Coast-Vancouver Island, which includes a list of the datasets, indicators, and benchmark values included in the analysis.

### Milestone 3: South Coast-Vancouver Island – draft assessments integrated into the Pacific Salmon Explorer (PSE) development website

**Estimated Cost CRF** $140,000
**Estimated Cost Other Funding Sources** $106,892
**Start and End dates:** 01-Oct-2019 to 31-Dec-2019

### General Description of the Milestone:

This milestone encompasses all activities related to integrating the results of the South Coast-Vancouver Island analytical work (2019-2020 Milestone 2) into the PSE development website. This development website will be for internal review purposes (including with the Technical Advisory Committees) and will not be available to the general public.

A development website for the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE, will be used to show draft assessment results and solicit feedback on draft content, data display, visual design, and usability from the Technical Advisory Committee and other project partners. The development website will also be used to share draft results with the Technical Advisory Committee for ground-truthing and QA/QC.

The development website will include clear and concise text that describes and provides the context for the data displayed, as well as information on methodologies and data sources used in the assessments for all of the habitat and population indicators.

A suite of interactive maps will be produced for each indicator showing low, moderate, or high risk of habitat degradation for each salmon Conservation Unit in the South Coast-Vancouver Island. In addition, the cumulative risk of habitat degradation will be visualized for spawning and migratory habitats for over 90 Conservation Units in the region. Interactive graphs displaying approximately sixty years of stock assessment data for each Conservation Unit will also be visualized in the PSE.

A centralized repository of vetted datasets related to salmon and their habitat in the South Coast-Vancouver Island, plus associated metadata records, will be made publicly available through the Salmon Data Library (data.salmonwatersheds.ca).

### Tasks:

1. Develop a schedule of activities and define expected partner contributions specific to this milestone
2. Ongoing engagement with the Technical Advisory Committee.
3. Integrate results of habitat risk assessments and population status assessments into an internal development website.
4. Develop written content for the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE.
5. Integrate datasets into the online Salmon Data Library for public access.

### Expected Results:

1. Access to the internal development website for the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE will be provided to DFO at the completion of this milestone.

### Milestone 4: South Coast-Vancouver Island – public release of assessments in the Pacific Salmon Explorer (PSE)

**Estimated Cost CRF** $70,000
**Estimated Cost Other Funding Sources** $106,892
**Start and End dates:** 01-Jan-2020 to 31-Mar-2020

### General Description of the Milestone:

This milestone is focussed on the public launch of the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE. PSF will work with the Technical Advisory Committee and other project partners to seek feedback on the draft development website (2019-2020 Milestone 3). A public launch of the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE is expected by March 2020, including announcements on psf.ca, on salmonwatersheds.ca, and in various social media platforms.

The South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE will undergo a rigorous QA/QC and be developed in a way that facilitates easy updates. In addition to viewing and interacting with maps and graphs, the PSE will provide end users with a number of ways to engage with data for the South Coast-Vancouver Island. This includes the ability to download raw datasets and print summary reports for individual Conservation Units directly from the tool.

### Tasks:

1. Develop a schedule of activities and define expected partner contributions specific to this milestone
2. Ongoing engagement with the Technical Advisory Committee.
3. Seek feedback on the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE and revise.
4. Undertake QA/QC of the South Coast-Vancouver Island section of the PSE.

### 4. Effect and Duration

**4.1.** This Agreement will come into effect on execution by both Parties and, unless terminated earlier in accordance with section 7, will terminate on March 31, 2020.

### 5. Amendment

**5.1.** The Parties may amend this Agreement at any time for any reason, but such amendment will have no force or effect unless made in writing and signed by both Parties.

**6.1.** The obligations of the Recipient under sections 2, 7, 9 and 11 of Schedule 2, and under subsections 1.3.1, 1.4, 1.5, 2.5 and 3.3 of Schedule 3 will survive the expiry or termination of this Agreement.

### 7. Termination

**7.1.** DFO may terminate this Agreement upon ten days written notice if it is determined that the Milestone(s) outlined in Schedule 5 are not being carried out or the deliverables and/or results outlined in Schedule 5 cannot be achieved. Upon termination of this Agreement the Recipient shall return to DFO any contributions, made by DFO, which are in excess of the eligible expenses incurred by the Recipient up to the date of notice.

**7.2.** The Recipient must inform DFO of any circumstances which may render the Milestone(s) no longer eligible and provide any documentation required to verify eligibility. **7.3.** This Agreement may be terminated by either Party with ten (10) days’ notice in writing given to the other Party.

You are an OCR data extraction expert.
You will be given:
1. A LAYOUT MAP (JSON) from a prior layout detection step, containing
   sections, fields, field types, bounding boxes, is_filled flags,
   optional legend_ref references, and a top-level legends array
2. The original form image

Your task: extract the VALUE of every field where is_filled = true,
using the bounding_box coordinates to locate each field precisely
in the image before reading it.

LAYOUT MAP: given as JSON, already extracted by a prior layout detection step. It contains a "sections" array, where each section has a "fields" array.

=== EXTRACTION RULES ===

GENERAL:
- Use bounding_box coordinates to locate each field in the image
  before extracting its value
- Only extract fields where is_filled = true
- Set value = null for all fields where is_filled = false
- Preserve section hierarchy from the layout map in your output
- Do not infer or guess values — only transcribe what is visible
- Do NOT re-extract or expand legends — they are already captured
  in the layout map and must not appear in this output

FIELD TYPE RULES:
- text_input / number_input: transcribe exactly as printed
- segmented_text_input (e.g. VRN, License #): return as single
  concatenated string, e.g. "123456"
- date_input / segmented_date_input: return as YYYY-MM-DD if
  determinable, otherwise transcribe as-is
- time_input_24hr: return as HH:MM
- checkbox: return true if checked/marked, false if empty
- radio_group / circled_option: return the selected option label
  as a string
- signature: return { "present": true, "handwritten": true }
- text_label / section_label / table_column_header: skip —
  these are structural, not data fields
- table_row / table_rows: return as array of objects using
  column headers as keys (see TABLE RULES)

HANDWRITTEN TEXT:
- Flag any handwritten value with "handwritten": true
- Transcribe exactly as written, including abbreviations

CONFIDENCE LEVELS:
- "high": clearly printed/typed, unambiguous
- "medium": partially legible, or inferred from context
- "low": unclear, obscured, ambiguous, or conflicting

TABLE RULES:
- Return each table as an array of row objects
- Use column header labels as keys
- Example:
  {
    "table_name": "TRAP STRING HAULED LOG",
    "rows": [
      {
        "Date": "2021/04/02",
        "Latitude": "47°03.10'N",
        "Longitude": "60°22.90'W",
        "Depth (fath.)": "75",
        "# of traps": "20",
        "Soak time (days)": "2",
        "Catch Kept (pounds)": "1000",
        "Comments": "bycatch of 2 scuplin"
      }
    ]
  }

=== OUTPUT FORMAT ===
{
  "form_title": "...",
  "layout_template_id": "...",
  "extracted_at": "ISO-8601 timestamp",
  "sections": [
    {
      "section_name": "...",
      "fields": [
        {
          "label": "...",
          "value": "...",
          "field_type": "...",
          "handwritten": false,
          "confidence": "high | medium | low"
        }
      ],
      "tables": [
        {
          "table_name": "...",
          "handwritten": "...",
          "rows": [ { "column_header": "value" } ]
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "flags": [
    {
      "type": "missing_required | low_confidence | illegible | unexpected_format | cross_field_inconsistency",
      "message": "Human-readable description of the issue",
      "field_label": "...",
      "section_name": "...",
      "bounding_box": { "x": 0, "y": 0, "width": 0, "height": 0 }
    }
  ]
}

FLAGS RULES:
Raise a flag for any of the following: missing required fields,
low-confidence extractions, illegible values, unexpected formats,
or cross-field inconsistencies (e.g. Date Sailed is after Date Landed)
For each flag, include:

"type": one of the categories above
"message": a clear, human-readable description of the issue
"field_label": the label of the affected field, carried forward
from the layout map
"section_name": the section containing the affected field,
carried forward from the layout map
"bounding_box": the bounding_box of the affected field copied
exactly from the layout map; omit this key only if no
bounding_box exists in the layout map for that field

For cross-field inconsistencies involving two fields, include the
bounding boxes of both under "bounding_boxes" (array) instead of
the singular "bounding_box" key
